title: Branched conformal structures and the Dyson superprocess
speaker: Govind Menon
affiliation: Brown University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: In the early 1920s, Loewner introduced a constructive approach to the Riemann mapping theorem that realized a conformal mapping as the solution to a differential equation. Roughly, the “input” to Loewner’s differential equation is a driving measure and the “output” is a family of nested, conformally equivalent domains. This theory was revitalized in the late 1990s by Schramm. The Schramm-Loewner evolution (SLE) is a stochastic family of slit mappings driven by Loewner’s equation when the driving measure is an atom executing Brownian motion.

proof_stages.label: Loewner-Herglotz conformal-map framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up Loewner evolution with a càdlàg family of driving measures, Herglotz functions, inverse maps, and hulls as a constructive mechanism for producing conformal maps.

proof_stages.label: Tree-valued conformal-growth target
proof_stages.summary: Motivating aggregation models and continuum-tree scaling theory lead to the aim of building a rigorous Loewner model whose hulls are embedded random trees.

proof_stages.label: Branching Dyson Brownian driving measure
proof_stages.summary: The model is defined by taking a critical binary Galton-Watson tree for births and deaths, evolving alive particles by Dyson Coulombic repulsion between branch times, and using the resulting empirical atomic measure as the Loewner driver.

proof_stages.label: Finite-time embedded-tree theorem
proof_stages.summary: In the no-spatial-noise case, each finite-time Loewner hull is a graph embedding of the original combinatorial tree with analytic edges and symmetric 120-degree triple junctions, a conclusion depending on the exact Dyson splitting rate.

proof_stages.label: Dyson superprocess limit and martingale problem
proof_stages.summary: After scaling the branching Dyson particle system, the proposed continuum driving measure is characterized by a measure-valued SPDE with Hilbert-transform transport and square-root white noise, and the corresponding martingale problem is stated to be well posed.

proof_stages.label: Origin of the two SPDE terms
proof_stages.summary: The scaling-limit equation is explained by combining the classical Feller limit for critical branching, which gives the square-root noise, with a Cauchy-transform identity for Dyson Coulomb repulsion, which gives the Hilbert-transform drift.

proof_stages.label: Limiting Loewner hulls and regularity obstacle
proof_stages.summary: The limiting measure can formally drive Loewner evolution and yields weak hull convergence, but proving that the limit hull is a genuine fractal tree requires density and hull-regularity estimates that remain open.

examples.name: Complex Burgers equation for positive density
examples.note: In response to a fluids question, the speaker notes that without noise and for positive density the Cauchy transform turns the Hilbert-transform equation into Burgers' equation in the complex plane.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker derives the Hilbert-transform/complex-Burgers structure by applying a Cauchy/Herglotz transform to the particle ODE and explaining the resulting equation

examples.name: Dawson-Watanabe superprocess comparison
